label: delphi-r1-p7
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

In the City of Rio Robles (invented), Councilmember Dana Okafor's spouse is the salaried Director of Programs (~$95,000/yr) at Harbor Light Alliance, a 501(c)(3) homeless-services provider. Under a 2019 city resolution, Okafor also sits on Harbor Light's board of directors, uncompensated, as the council's designated liaison.

The council is about to (a) adopt the framework and scoring criteria for a competitive RFP allocating $2.3M in combined city general-fund and state HHAP pass-through money for homelessness services, and then (b) award the resulting contract(s). Harbor Light is one of three realistic bidders and has historically won about 60% of the city's homelessness contracts. The spouse's position is funded out of Harbor Light's general operating budget, to which city contracts contribute roughly 40%.

Okafor asks the city attorney: May I vote on the RFP framework? On the award? Must I resign the board seat or must my spouse's employment change? And if I simply recuse, is the city safe contracting with Harbor Light?

## The question

Analyze under Government Code § 1090 (including whether board membership plus a spouse's salaried employment is a prohibited financial interest 'in' the contract, whether any § 1091 remote-interest or § 1091.5 noninterest exception applies, and whether recusal even cures § 1090 for a board member), and under the Political Reform Act (§§ 87100, 87103 — community-property interest in spouse's income, materiality, and the public-generally exception), plus common-law conflict doctrine. What combination of resignation, recusal, wall procedures, or contract structuring lets the city lawfully fund Harbor Light, if any? What are the consequences of getting it wrong (§ 1090 voidness, disgorgement, criminal exposure, FPPC enforcement)?

## Proposition to assess

"If the city awards the homelessness-services contract to Harbor Light Alliance while Okafor holds the board seat and her spouse holds her position — even with Okafor fully recused from the RFP and the award — the contract would be void or voidable under Government Code § 1090."

Estimate the probability (0–100) that this proposition is correct, with your reasoning. rationales = your independent load-bearing reasons, one sentence each. authorities = specific authorities you are actually relying on (they will be flagged for human verification). biggest_uncertainty = the single thing you are least sure of.

Analytical starting point for you: Begin from the advice a cautious public-agency counsel would give, then ask whether caution is legally required or merely prudent.
text: {"probability":67,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"More likely than not correct: recusal probably does NOT cure the § 1090 problem here, because the facts (spouse's salary funded ~40% by city contracts, Harbor Light's ~60% historical win rate) point toward a direct/prohibited \"financial interest\" imputed to Okafor through her spouse's employment, not a merely \"remote interest\" under § 1091 that disclosure-and-abstention could validate. Under Thomson v. Call, a prohibited § 1090 interest held by a sitting member of the contracting body cannot be cured by that member's recusal from the vote — the taint runs to the whole body/contract, not just the individual's ballot. Okafor's own uncompensated board seat may independently qualify as a § 1091.5 \"noninterest\" (nonprofit-liaison exception), which would remove one prong of the problem, but that exception doesn't reach the separate and more serious spousal-salary interest. The main source of doubt is genuine ambiguity over whether the spousal employment interest is properly \"remote\" (fixed salary not tied to any one contract, arguably analogous to § 1091(b)(2)) rather than \"direct,\" which would flip the analysis and let a well-executed recusal-plus-disclosure procedure save the contract.","rationales":["Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633 holds that a disqualifying § 1090 interest held by a sitting member of the contracting body is not cured by that member's abstention from the vote, because the statutory prohibition runs to the whole body, not just the individual's ballot.","The spouse's ~$95,000 salary is community property (Fam. Code § 760) and California courts construe 'financially interested' under § 1090 broadly, resolving doubts in favor of disqualification (e.g., Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565), supporting imputation of the spouse's interest to Okafor.","The facts emphasize that Harbor Light's general operating budget (which funds the spouse's salary) is ~40% city-contract-derived and that Harbor Light wins ~60% of these awards, a close enough nexus between this specific $2.3M RFP and the spouse's job/compensation to plausibly push the interest out of § 1091's 'remote interest' category and into a direct, non-curable interest.","Even if Okafor's own uncompensated board seat independently qualifies as a § 1091.5 'noninterest' for noncompensated officers of an affiliated/liaison nonprofit, that exception is narrow and would not immunize the distinct spousal-employment financial interest, which is the more serious defect.","A credible counterargument exists that the spouse's fixed salary (not contingent on any single contract's award) could instead be framed as a § 1091(b)-type remote interest curable by disclosure-plus-abstention, which is the main reason the probability is not higher than roughly two-thirds."],"authorities":["Gov. Code § 1090","Gov. Code § 1091 (remote interests)","Gov. Code § 1091.5 (noninterests, incl. noncompensated nonprofit-officer/liaison provision — subsection/exact text needs verification)","Gov. Code § 1092 (voidness remedy)","Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633","Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565","People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289","Fraser-Yamor Agency, Inc. v. County of Del Norte (1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201","Family Code § 760 (community property)"],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether Okafor's (and her spouse's) interest correctly classifies as a \"remote interest\" under § 1091(b) — curable by disclosure and full abstention — versus a direct/prohibited financial interest to which Thomson v. Call's no-cure-by-recusal rule applies; this classification is outcome-determinative and I am not fully certain of the precise statutory text/scope of § 1091(b)'s employee-compensation carve-out or § 1091.5's nonprofit-liaison noninterest provision as applied to these specific facts."}

data.position_summary: More likely than not correct: recusal probably does NOT cure the § 1090 problem here, because the facts (spouse's salary funded ~40% by city contracts, Harbor Light's ~60% historical win rate) point toward a direct/prohibited "financial interest" imputed to Okafor through her spouse's employment, not a merely "remote interest" under § 1091 that disclosure-and-abstention could validate. Under Thomson v. Call, a prohibited § 1090 interest held by a sitting member of the contracting body cannot be cured by that member's recusal from the vote — the taint runs to the whole body/contract, not just the individual's ballot. Okafor's own uncompensated board seat may independently qualify as a § 1091.5 "noninterest" (nonprofit-liaison exception), which would remove one prong of the problem, but that exception doesn't reach the separate and more serious spousal-salary interest. The main source of doubt is genuine ambiguity over whether the spousal employment interest is properly "remote" (fixed salary not tied to any one contract, arguably analogous to § 1091(b)(2)) rather than "direct," which would flip the analysis and let a well-executed recusal-plus-disclosure procedure save the contract.
rationales: Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633 holds that a disqualifying § 1090 interest held by a sitting member of the contracting body is not cured by that member's abstention from the vote, because the statutory prohibition runs to the whole body, not just the individual's ballot.

The spouse's ~$95,000 salary is community property (Fam. Code § 760) and California courts construe 'financially interested' under § 1090 broadly, resolving doubts in favor of disqualification (e.g., Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565), supporting imputation of the spouse's interest to Okafor.

The facts emphasize that Harbor Light's general operating budget (which funds the spouse's salary) is ~40% city-contract-derived and that Harbor Light wins ~60% of these awards, a close enough nexus between this specific $2.3M RFP and the spouse's job/compensation to plausibly push the interest out of § 1091's 'remote interest' category and into a direct, non-curable interest.

Even if Okafor's own uncompensated board seat independently qualifies as a § 1091.5 'noninterest' for noncompensated officers of an affiliated/liaison nonprofit, that exception is narrow and would not immunize the distinct spousal-employment financial interest, which is the more serious defect.

A credible counterargument exists that the spouse's fixed salary (not contingent on any single contract's award) could instead be framed as a § 1091(b)-type remote interest curable by disclosure-plus-abstention, which is the main reason the probability is not higher than roughly two-thirds.
authorities: Gov. Code § 1091.5 (noninterests, incl. noncompensated nonprofit-officer/liaison provision — subsection/exact text needs verification)
Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565
Fraser-Yamor Agency, Inc. v. County of Del Norte (1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether Okafor's (and her spouse's) interest correctly classifies as a "remote interest" under § 1091(b) — curable by disclosure and full abstention — versus a direct/prohibited financial interest to which Thomson v. Call's no-cure-by-recusal rule applies; this classification is outcome-determinative and I am not fully certain of the precise statutory text/scope of § 1091(b)'s employee-compensation carve-out or § 1091.5's nonprofit-liaison noninterest provision as applied to these specific facts.
